metadata.summary: Cloud Run Jobs for batch processing: parallel task execution, CLOUD_RUN_TASK_INDEX work distribution, idempotency, checkpointing, Cloud Scheduler integration, and Terraform configuration.

content.title: Cloud Run Jobs
content.summary: Cloud Run Jobs run containers to completion rather than serving HTTP requests. Jobs support up to 10,000 parallel tasks, 168-hour timeouts, and automatic retries. Use CLOUD_RUN_TASK_INDEX and CLOUD_RUN_TASK_COUNT environment variables to distribute work across parallel tasks. Tasks MUST be idempotent because retries re-execute the same task.

why.p: Cloud Run Services are optimized for HTTP request/response workloads that auto-scale on traffic. Jobs are optimized for run-to-completion batch tasks: ETL pipelines, report generation, data exports, ML training data preparation. Jobs handle parallelism natively (up to 10,000 tasks) without requiring external orchestration. The 168-hour timeout accommodates multi-day batch jobs that would time out in any HTTP-based service.

list.item: Batch processing large datasets that can be partitioned across parallel tasks.
ETL pipelines, data exports, report generation, and scheduled data transformations.
One-off administrative tasks (database migrations, cleanup jobs, backfills).
ML training data preparation or model evaluation pipelines.
Recurring scheduled tasks (replace Cloud Functions cron triggers for complex jobs).
HTTP-triggered request processing — use Cloud Run Services.
Streaming or continuous processing — use Dataflow or Pub/Sub push subscriptions.
Workflows with complex DAG dependencies — use Cloud Workflows or Composer.
Jobs needing more than 8 vCPU or 32 GB memory per task — use GKE Batch or Dataproc.

rule.statement: Tasks MUST be idempotent: running the same task twice with the same CLOUD_RUN_TASK_INDEX MUST produce the same result and not corrupt state. Set max-retries to 3 (default) for transient failures and 0 for non-idempotent tasks where retry is dangerous. Always set task-timeout explicitly — default is 10 minutes; ETL tasks often need hours.
rule.rationale: Cloud Run retries failed tasks up to max-retries. If tasks are not idempotent, retries cause duplicate writes, double-charged payments, or corrupted state. Explicit timeout prevents infinite-hang tasks from consuming quota indefinitely.

example.title: Basic job creation and execution
example.code:
# Create job gcloud run jobs create my-job \ --image=us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/my-project/my-repo/my-job:v1 \ --region=us-central1 \ --task-timeout=600 \ --max-retries=3
# Execute job gcloud run jobs execute my-job --region=us-central1
# Execute with overrides gcloud run jobs execute my-job \ --region=us-central1 \ --update-env-vars=BATCH_SIZE=1000

example.title: Parallel job with 100 tasks
example.note: --tasks=100 creates 100 task instances. --parallelism=10 runs 10 simultaneously. Each task receives CLOUD_RUN_TASK_INDEX (0-99) and CLOUD_RUN_TASK_COUNT (100).

rule.statement: Use CLOUD_RUN_TASK_INDEX (0-based) and CLOUD_RUN_TASK_COUNT to distribute work across parallel tasks. Divide the total work set into equal slices by task index. The last task MUST handle the remainder when total_items is not divisible by task_count. Use checkpointing to Cloud Storage for tasks exceeding 30 minutes to survive potential task restarts.
rule.rationale: Without work distribution by index, all tasks would process the same data. The index-based slice pattern is the canonical Cloud Run Jobs pattern for parallel batch: deterministic, idempotent, no coordination required between tasks.

example.title: Python task index work distribution
example.code:
import os
def process_batch(): task_index = int(os.environ.get('CLOUD_RUN_TASK_INDEX', 0)) task_count = int(os.environ.get('CLOUD_RUN_TASK_COUNT', 1))
# Calculate work slice total_items = 10000 items_per_task = total_items // task_count start = task_index * items_per_task end = start + items_per_task
if task_index == task_count - 1: end = total_items # Last task handles remainder
print(f"Task {task_index}: Processing items {start} to {end}")
# Process items for i in range(start, end): process_item(i)

antipattern.description: Processing all items in every task without using CLOUD_RUN_TASK_INDEX for partitioning.
antipattern.reason: All tasks process the same data N times. With 100 tasks and 10,000 items, 1,000,000 items are processed instead of 10,000. Results are duplicated N times; cost multiplies by task_count.

rule.statement: Use Cloud Scheduler to trigger Cloud Run Jobs on a recurring cron schedule. The scheduler service account MUST have roles/run.invoker on the job. Set attempt-deadline on the scheduler job to match the expected job duration plus buffer (not the task timeout). Use the v1 jobs run API endpoint for HTTP POST triggering.
rule.rationale: Cloud Scheduler handles cron expression parsing, timezone conversion, and retry-on-failure for the trigger itself (not the job tasks). The attempt-deadline controls how long Scheduler waits for the HTTP response before retrying the trigger — it must be long enough for the job to start (not complete).

example.title: Create job and Cloud Scheduler trigger
example.code:
# Create job gcloud run jobs create daily-report \ --image=us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/my-project/my-repo/report:v1 \ --region=us-central1 \ --task-timeout=1800
# Create scheduler gcloud scheduler jobs create http daily-report-trigger \ --location=us-central1 \ --schedule="0 6 * * *" \ --time-zone="Europe/Kyiv" \ --uri="https://us-central1-run.googleapis.com/apis/run.googleapis.com/v1/namespaces/my-project/jobs/daily-report:run" \ --oauth-service-account-email=scheduler-sa@my-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com

example.title: Terraform: Cloud Run Job with Cloud Scheduler
Cloud Run Job YAML for gcloud apply
example.code: apiVersion: run.googleapis.com/v1 kind: Job metadata: name: batch-processor spec: template: spec: taskCount: 100 parallelism: 10 template: spec: maxRetries: 3 timeoutSeconds: 3600 containers: - image: us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/my-project/my-repo/processor:v1 resources: limits: cpu: "2" memory: 4Gi env: - name: BUCKET value: my-data-bucket

checklist.check: Task count and parallelism configured appropriately for data volume.
Task timeout set explicitly (default 10min is often too short for ETL).
Retry count configured (default 3; set to 0 for non-idempotent tasks).
Memory and CPU allocated to match per-task processing needs.
Tasks are idempotent (safe to retry with same CLOUD_RUN_TASK_INDEX).
Checkpointing implemented for tasks exceeding 30 minutes.
CLOUD_RUN_TASK_INDEX used for work distribution across tasks.
CLOUD_RUN_TASK_COUNT used for total work calculation.
Error handling and structured logging implemented.
Cloud Scheduler job created with correct cron and service account (if scheduled).
Scheduler service account has roles/run.invoker on the job.
Timezone configured on Cloud Scheduler (do not rely on default UTC).
